notes.note: "Teucris" is one of Cicero's code-names for a confidential business or political matter (it recurs at 1.13.6 and 1.14.7); the real referent is not securely identifiable. The grammatically feminine "that woman" may be part of the disguise rather than literal.

"Titus" here is most likely a freedman or agent employed in the Teucris business, not Atticus (Titus Pomponius Atticus), whom Cicero would not refer to in the third person mid-sentence. The identity is uncertain.

Latin "minore centesimis": the centesima usurae was 1 percent per month, i.e. 12 percent per year, the legal maximum for interest at Rome. Cicero complains his connections cannot borrow even at that ceiling rate.

notes.anchor: the praetor will move to the people
notes.note: A praetor could put a proposal (rogatio) before the popular assembly. The point is that Antonius's recall from his province would be pressed both by Pompey and by a praetor's bill.
Pompey divorced Mucia Tertia (half-sister of the Metelli Celer and Nepos) on his return from the East in 62 BC, reportedly for adultery; the divorce alienated the Metelli from him.

notes.anchor: caught in women's dress at Gaius Caesar's house when the rite \textit{pro populo} was being held
notes.note: The rite "for the people" (pro populo) was the December festival of the Bona Dea, the Good Goddess, from which all males were rigorously excluded; it was held in the house of a magistrate with imperium, here Caesar's (he was praetor and pontifex maximus), and conducted by the women and the Vestals. Clodius's intrusion in a woman's dress was therefore a grave sacrilege.

notes.anchor: The Kalends of January, in the consulship of Marcus Messalla and Marcus Piso
notes.note: Roman years were dated by the two consuls; M. Valerius Messalla Niger and M. Pupius Piso were consuls of 61 BC. The Kalends was the first day of the month, so this fixes the letter to 1 January 61 BC.
